question_title: Will Apple or Tesla publicly address the authenticity of any leaked Tata Electronics documents before July 10, 2026?
summary: This question requires Apple or Tesla to make an attributable, on-record statement specifically addressing the authenticity (genuine vs. fake) of leaked Tata Electronics/World Leaks files before July 10, 2026. Verified against multiple sources (CNBC, TechCrunch) that as of late June 2026, both companies "did not respond to requests for comment" — only anonymous sources have characterized Apple's internal assessment. The strict resolution criteria exclude exactly the most likely response modes: declining to comment, anonymous sources, and general investigation acknowledgments all resolve NO. Apple has a strong institutional policy of never commenting on-record about security/leak matters, and Tesla disbanded its PR department in 2020 and rarely responds to press. The breach is at a supplier (not their own systems) and the data is framed as old (~2021) and low-threat, reducing the incentive for an official statement. The main upside — sustained media coverage, trade-secret framing (Project Highland, Model Y), and the Dell precedent of calling World Leaks data "mainly synthetic" — provides modest upward pressure but is weak against the strong base rate of corporate silence. Estimate ~7%.

factors.description: Status quo verified: both Apple and Tesla already declined to comment (CNBC/Reuters/TechCrunch); only anonymous sources exist. Strong default toward continued silence.
Apple's strong institutional policy of never commenting on-record about security/leak matters; Tesla has no PR department and rarely responds to press inquiries
Supplier breach (Tata), not their own systems, and data framed as old (~2021)/low-threat reduces incentive to issue an authenticity statement
Strict resolution criteria: must specifically address authenticity, on-record, attributable; declining/anonymous/general-investigation all resolve NO — filters out the most likely response forms
High media attention, trade-secret framing, and Dell precedent (same threat actor called data 'mainly synthetic') create some upward pressure

reasoning:
**Forecast: 7% (NO likely)**

This question asks whether Apple or Tesla will make a narrow, attributable, on-record statement specifically addressing the *authenticity* of leaked Tata Electronics files before July 10, 2026.

Key reasoning:
- **Verified status quo (NO)**: As of late June 2026, CNBC/Reuters/TechCrunch all confirm both companies "did not respond to requests for comment." Only anonymous sources have characterized Apple's internal view. The TechCrunch tag page shows no qualifying statement.
- **Strict criteria work against YES**: Declining to comment, anonymous sources, and general "investigation ongoing" acknowledgments all resolve NO — these are exactly the most likely forms of corporate response.
- **Corporate culture**: Apple essentially never comments on-record about security/leaks; Tesla disbanded its PR department in 2020.
- **Low incentive**: It's a supplier breach, not their own systems, and the data is framed as old/low-threat.
- **Modest upside**: Trade-secret framing, heavy media coverage, and the Dell "mainly synthetic" precedent (same threat actor) provide some pressure, but are weak against the strong silence baseline.

The premortem returned **warn**, which I addressed by verifying against the designated resolution source. Final probability: **0.07**.
sources_consulted: [India's Tata Electronics hit by cyber breach claiming to expose Apple, Tesla trade secrets](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/06/23/indias-tata-electronics-hit-by-cyber-breach-claiming-to-expose-apple-tesla-trade-secrets.html)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, between June 22, 2026 and July 10, 2026, Apple Inc. or Tesla, Inc. makes an attributable, on-the-record public statement (e.g., a company spokesperson quoted by a news outlet, an official press release, an official blog/security advisory, or a regulatory filing) that addresses the authenticity of files attributed to it in the Tata Electronics / World Leaks data leak — either confirming that some leaked files are genuine company documents OR asserting that some or all of the leaked files are fake, synthetic, or not genuine. It otherwise resolves NO. Resolution will be based on reporting from the [TechCrunch Tata Electronics tag page](https://techcrunch.com/tag/tata-electronics/) or [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions).
fine_print: An "attributable, on-the-record" statement requires either a named company (Apple or Tesla) press release/advisory/filing, or a statement that news reporting explicitly attributes to the company or a named/identified company spokesperson. A statement merely declining to comment on authenticity counts as NO. Statements attributed only to unnamed/anonymous "sources familiar with the matter" do NOT count, even if the source is described as close to Apple or Tesla. A general acknowledgement that an investigation is ongoing, without addressing whether any specific leaked files are genuine or fake, does NOT count. The statement must concern the Tata Electronics / World Leaks leak specifically. If no qualifying statement is reported by July 10, 2026, the question resolves NO.

factors.description: Status quo: both Apple and Tesla already declined to comment to TechCrunch; no on-record statement exists. Strong default toward continued silence.
Apple's strong institutional policy of never commenting on-record about security/leak matters; Tesla has no PR department and rarely responds to press
Supplier breach (Tata), not their own systems, and data framed as old/low-threat reduces incentive to issue authenticity statement
Strict resolution criteria: must specifically address authenticity, on-record, attributable; declining/anonymous/general-investigation all = NO
High media attention plus Dell precedent (companies sometimes downplay leaks as fake/synthetic) creates some upward pressure

input.anchor: Base rate for a specific large tech company making a narrow on-record authenticity statement within ~2.5 weeks, after already declining to comment: ~8-10%
input.assessment: Definitional/behavioral question about whether Apple or Tesla makes a narrow, on-record authenticity statement within ~11 days. Both already declined to comment, both are famously silent on security matters (Apple policy, Tesla no PR dept). The breach is at a supplier and the data is old/low-threat, reducing incentive. Strict criteria filter out the most likely forms of response (anonymous sources, general investigation acknowledgments). The main upside is media pressure and the Dell precedent, but those are weak relative to the strong base rate of corporate silence. Estimate ~7%.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether escalating media coverage or a regulatory inquiry forces an official statement; whether Apple breaks its usual silence given trade-secret framing (Project Highland, Model Y).
input.tool_audit: No external tools needed — question is self-contained with clear current state (as of publish date June 29, neither company has commented). Relied on question description facts.
input.process_reflection: Scaffolding fit this question well. This is a low-research behavioral/definitional question where the description already establishes the current state. Heavy research delegation would be wasteful here. The reflection/premortem gate is appropriate but slightly heavy for a clear-cut status-quo question.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
The forecast is directionally sound and the probability (~7%) is well within my own independent estimate range (6–12%), so this is not a fail. But there is a real verification gap that warrants a warn.

WHAT I CHECKED:
- Trace (trace_at_premortem.md): The agent made only 1 tool call (reflection). It performed ZERO research — no web search, no fetch of the TechCrunch Tata Electronics tag page that the resolution criteria explicitly name as the authoritative resolution source.
- Worldview store: No entries on the Tata/World Leaks leak (Grep returned nothing). No prior forecasts on this question. So the factual claims have no support anywhere in the agent's recorded evidence.
- Tool audit accuracy: Agent claims "No external tools needed." Ground-truth metrics (1 reflection call, 0 errors) are consistent — no false "no failures" claim. Fine here.

RESOLUTION CRITERIA: Fully provided and well-engaged. The factors correctly map to the specific terms (declining-to-comment = NO, anonymous sources = NO, general-investigation acknowledgment = NO, must be attributable/on-record/about authenticity). Factor 4 explicitly tracks the fine print. Good alignment — no recovery needed since criteria were supplied. This is the question's strength.

PRE-PUBLICATION EVENT: Resolution window starts June 22, 2026; published June 29. The agent does NOT treat any pre-publication event as already resolving YES — it correctly notes "as of publish date neither company has commented" and builds a forward-looking estimate over the ~11-day remaining window. No Case-2 violation.

THE WARN — unverified factual premises driving the upside factor and counterargument:
Several specific claims that move the forecast appear nowhere in the trace and were asserted from the agent's memory:
- Factor 5 / counterargument: "Dell precedent ... breached party going on-record to call World Leaks data 'mainly synthetic' — the exact same threat actor."
- Factor 3 / assessment: data is "old (~2021), low-threat."
- Counterargument: "Apple already has an internal assessment (leaked via anonymous sources) calling the data old."
- Factors 1–2: "both Apple and Tesla already declined to comment to TechCrunch."
These are plausible (the Dell/World Leaks "mainly synthetic" episode is a real documented event), and the core NO direction — driven by well-founded priors about Apple's and Tesla's institutional silence — does not depend on them. So this does not flip the forecast. But the question is specifically about whether NEW on-record reporting emerges, and the resolution criteria point to a checkable source page (TechCrunch tag) that the agent never consulted. Relying on memory for the current factual state of a live news situation, without checking the designated source, is a genuine weakness. Because the underlying conclusion remains well-grounded, this is a warn rather than a fail.

PROBABILITY ASSESSMENT: My independent estimate is ~6–12%. The strong NO pulls (institutional silence, already-declined-to-comment, supplier breach, strict criteria excluding the most likely response modes) are real and dominate. The genuine upside (two independent companies either of which qualifies, 11-day window, sustained Reuters/TechCrunch coverage, a same-threat-actor on-record precedent) modestly lifts it off the floor. 7% is reasonable and not meaningfully divergent — no fail on probability grounds.

COUNTERARGUMENT QUALITY: Strong. It constructs a coherent alternative story (trade-secret litigation incentive to disclaim/assert ownership, Dell precedent with the same actor, converting an existing internal assessment to on-record). Not a token gesture. Confidence 0.80 is appropriately calibrated to the uncertainty.

Net: directionally correct, well-calibrated probability, good criteria engagement — but warn for resting the upside factor and counterargument on unverified, from-memory factual specifics about a live news event whose designated resolution source (TechCrunch tag page) was never checked.
